screens.title: TMUA index questions are rarely just index questions
screens.body: In the TMUA, index laws often appear inside a larger problem. You may need to rewrite roots, split fractions, or simplify powers before you can differentiate, compare values, or spot a structure.
screens.keyPoint: Useful rewrites: √x = x1/2, x√x = x3/2, and 1xn = x-n.

screens.title: Rewrite before you manipulate

steps.title: Start with the denominator
steps.body: If you see x√x, rewrite it as x × x<sup>1/2</sup> = x<sup>3/2</sup>.

screens.question: For x > 0, which expression is equal to (x<sup>1/2</sup> + x<sup>-1/2</sup>)<sup>2</sup>?
screens.prompt: Expand the bracket, then simplify each power.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Squaring gives x<sup>1</sup> + 2x<sup>1/2</sup>x<sup>-1/2</sup> + x<sup>-1</sup>. The middle product is 2x<sup>0</sup> = 2, so the result is x + 2 + x<sup>-1</sup>.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Use (a + b)<sup>2</sup> = a<sup>2</sup> + 2ab + b<sup>2</sup>. Here, x<sup>1/2</sup>x<sup>-1/2</sup> = x<sup>0</sup> = 1, so the middle term becomes 2.

screens.title: Indices handled.
screens.body: You have taken a messy expression, rewritten it with powers, and simplified it term by term. Next up: surds.

screens.title: Surds are exact values, not decimals
screens.body: In TMUA questions, surds are often used because they keep values exact. You should be comfortable simplifying surds, combining like surds, and rationalising denominators without reaching for a calculator.
screens.keyPoint: A common move is taking out square factors: √72 = √(36 × 2) = 6√2. You can only combine like surds, so 3√2 + 5√2 = 8√2, but √2 + √3 cannot be simplified.

screens.title: Rationalising the denominator

steps.title: The aim
steps.body: Rationalising means rewriting a fraction so there is no surd left in the denominator.

steps.title: Simple case
steps.body: For 5√3, multiply the top and bottom by √3.

steps.title: Harder case
steps.body: If the denominator has two terms, like 2 + √3, multiply by the conjugate 2 - √3.

screens.question: Which of the following is equal to 62 + √3?
screens.prompt: Use the conjugate of the denominator.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Multiply by 2 - √3. The denominator becomes (2 + √3)(2 - √3) = 1, so the expression becomes 6(2 - √3) = 12 - 6√3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. The conjugate of 2 + √3 is 2 - √3. The denominator becomes 4 - 3 = 1, so the result is 6(2 - √3) = 12 - 6√3.

screens.title: Surds done.
screens.body: You have practised exact surd manipulation and rationalising denominators. Next up, and finally for this section: polynomials.

screens.title: Polynomials are about structure
screens.body: For TMUA algebra, polynomials often appear with hidden information: a known factor, a remainder, an unknown constant, or a root. The key is knowing what substitution tells you.
screens.keyPoint: If a polynomial f(x) is divided by x - a, the remainder is f(a). If f(a) = 0, then x - a is a factor.

screens.title: Remainder theorem and factor theorem

steps.title: Start with the divisor
steps.body: If a polynomial is divided by x - a, substitute x = a into the polynomial.

steps.title: Remainder theorem
steps.body: The remainder when f(x) is divided by x - a is f(a). For example, if dividing by x - 2, the remainder is f(2).

steps.title: Factor theorem
steps.body: The factor theorem is a specific case of the remainder theorem. If f(a) = 0, the remainder is zero, so x - a is a factor.

steps.title: Unknown constants
steps.body: If a question says the remainder is 7, set f(a) = 7. If it says x - a is a factor, set f(a) = 0.

screens.title: Indices, Surds and Polynomials: key points
keyPoints: Rewrite roots and denominators using powers when it makes algebra easier.
Split algebraic fractions before simplifying term by term.
Surds are exact values and should often be simplified rather than decimalised.
To rationalise a denominator with two terms, multiply by the conjugate.
The remainder when f(x) is divided by x - a is f(a).
The factor theorem is the zero-remainder case of the remainder theorem.
reviewItems: Practise simplifying expressions involving x√x and negative powers.
Practise rationalising denominators with conjugates.
Practise using f(a) to handle polynomial remainders and factors.
Next lesson: Quadratics and Inequalities.

screens.title: Quadratics can be read in different ways
screens.body: In TMUA questions, a quadratic is often useful because different forms reveal different information. Expanded form shows coefficients, factorised form shows roots, and completed-square form shows the turning point.
screens.keyPoint: For example, x<sup>2</sup> - 6x + 5 = (x - 1)(x - 5) = (x - 3)<sup>2</sup> - 4. Each form tells you something different.

screens.title: Read information from the useful form

steps.title: Factorise to find the roots
steps.body: x<sup>2</sup> - 6x + 5 = (x - 1)(x - 5), so the roots are x = 1 and x = 5.

steps.title: Read the turning point
steps.body: From (x - 3)<sup>2</sup> - 4, the turning point is (3, -4). Since the coefficient of x<sup>2</sup> is positive, this is a minimum.

screens.question: What is the minimum value of f(x) = 2x<sup>2</sup> - 8x + 3?
screens.prompt: Complete the square, remembering to factor out 2 first.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. 2x<sup>2</sup> - 8x + 3 = 2(x - 2)<sup>2</sup> - 5. Since the squared term cannot be negative, the minimum value is -5.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Factor out 2 from the first two terms: 2(x<sup>2</sup> - 4x) + 3. Then complete the square to get 2(x - 2)<sup>2</sup> - 5.

screens.title: You can now read a quadratic.
screens.body: You have used different forms of the same quadratic to find roots, turning points, and graph behaviour. Next up: hidden quadratics.

screens.title: Some equations are quadratic in disguise
screens.body: A hidden quadratic appears when an equation is not written directly as ax<sup>2</sup> + bx + c = 0, but can become a quadratic after a substitution. A common example is replacing x<sup>2</sup> with u.
screens.keyPoint: If you see x<sup>4</sup>, x<sup>2</sup>, and a constant, try u = x<sup>2</sup>. Then x<sup>4</sup> becomes u<sup>2</sup>.

screens.title: Use substitution to reveal the quadratic
screens.question: Solve x<sup>4</sup> - 5x<sup>2</sup> + 4 = 0.

steps.title: Spot the repeated structure
steps.body: The equation contains x<sup>4</sup> and x<sup>2</sup>. Since x<sup>4</sup> = (x<sup>2</sup>)<sup>2</sup>, use the substitution u = x<sup>2</sup>.

steps.title: Substitute back
steps.body: Since u = x<sup>2</sup>, we get x<sup>2</sup> = 1 or x<sup>2</sup> = 4.

steps.title: Find all real solutions
steps.body: x<sup>2</sup> = 1 gives x = ±1. x<sup>2</sup> = 4 gives x = ±2.

screens.finalAnswer: The solutions are x = -2, -1, 1, 2, so the correct option is A.

screens.question: How many real solutions does x<sup>4</sup> - 13x<sup>2</sup> + 36 = 0 have?
screens.prompt: Use u = x², then remember to convert back to x.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Let u = x<sup>2</sup>. Then u<sup>2</sup> - 13u + 36 = 0, so (u - 4)(u - 9) = 0. Therefore x<sup>2</sup> = 4 or x<sup>2</sup> = 9, giving x = ±2 or ±3. That is 4 real solutions.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Let u = x<sup>2</sup>. Then u<sup>2</sup> - 13u + 36 = 0, so u = 4 or u = 9. That gives x = ±2 and x = ±3, so there are 4 real solutions.

screens.title: Hidden quadratics unlocked.
screens.body: You have seen how a fourth-degree equation can secretly become a normal quadratic. Finally, let’s use quadratics to solve inequalities.

screens.title: Quadratic inequalities are about regions
screens.body: Solving a quadratic equation gives boundary points. Solving a quadratic inequality asks which regions are above or below zero. The roots split the number line into intervals.
screens.keyPoint: If (x - 2)(x - 5) > 0, the expression is positive outside the roots: x < 2 or x > 5.

screens.title: Compare a quadratic and a line
screens.question: For which values of x is x² - 4x + 3 > 2x - 5?

steps.title: Find the boundary points
steps.body: The expression is zero at x = 2 and x = 4. These split the number line into regions.

steps.title: Choose the correct regions
steps.body: Since the quadratic opens upwards, it is positive outside the roots.

screens.finalAnswer: The correct answer is A: x < 2 or x > 4.

screens.title: Quadratics and Inequalities: key points
keyPoints: Different forms of a quadratic reveal different information.
Factorised form shows roots.
Completed-square form shows the turning point.
Hidden quadratics can often be solved using a substitution such as u = x<sup>2</sup>.
After solving for u, always substitute back to find the actual x-values.
Quadratic inequalities are solved by finding boundary points and choosing regions.
reviewItems: Practise switching between factorised and completed-square form.
Review hidden quadratic substitutions like u = x<sup>2</sup>.
Practise solving inequalities by using the shape of the graph.
Next lesson: Functions and Simultaneous Systems.

screens.title: Composite functions are substitution in order
screens.body: Composite functions are useful because they train the same skill needed for harder simultaneous equations: replacing one expression with another. In f(g(x)), the inside function happens first.
screens.keyPoint: f(g(x)) means put the whole expression g(x) into f. The order matters: f(g(x)) and g(f(x)) are usually different.

screens.title: Work from the inside out

steps.title: Substitute into f
steps.body: Since f(x) = x<sup>2</sup> - 1, replace x with the whole expression 2x + 3.

screens.question: Let f(x) = x<sup>2</sup> - 1 and g(x) = 2x + 3. What is g(f(x))?
screens.prompt: Be careful: this is the opposite order to f(g(x)).
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. g(f(x)) means put f(x) into g. Since g(x) = 2x + 3, we get g(f(x)) = 2(x<sup>2</sup> - 1) + 3 = 2x<sup>2</sup> + 1.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. g(f(x)) means put f(x) into g, not the other way round. So g(f(x)) = 2(x<sup>2</sup> - 1) + 3 = 2x<sup>2</sup> + 1.

screens.title: Composite functions handled.
screens.body: You have practised substituting whole expressions into other rules. That same idea now appears in simultaneous equations.

screens.title: Substitution turns two equations into one
screens.body: The TMUA specification includes solving simultaneous equations analytically by substitution. A common case is one linear equation and one quadratic equation.
screens.keyPoint: Use the linear equation to replace one variable in the quadratic equation. This usually creates a quadratic equation in one variable.

screens.title: Start with a linear and quadratic system

steps.title: Start with the equations
steps.body: Suppose y = x + 3 and y = x<sup>2</sup> - 2x - 1.

steps.title: Set the expressions equal
steps.body: Both expressions equal y, so at an intersection we have x + 3 = x<sup>2</sup> - 2x - 1.

steps.title: Substitute back
steps.body: Using y = x + 3: if x = 4, y = 7. If x = -1, y = 2.

screens.question: The graphs y = 2x + 1 and y = x<sup>2</sup> - 2 intersect at two points. What are they?
screens.prompt: Set the two expressions for y equal, then substitute each x-value back into y = 2x + 1.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Setting the expressions equal gives x<sup>2</sup> - 2 = 2x + 1, so x<sup>2</sup> - 2x - 3 = 0. Factorising gives (x - 3)(x + 1) = 0, so x = 3 or x = -1. Substituting into y = 2x + 1 gives the points (3, 7) and (-1, -1).
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Set x<sup>2</sup> - 2 = 2x + 1. Rearranging gives x<sup>2</sup> - 2x - 3 = 0, which factorises as (x - 3)(x + 1). Then substitute both x-values into y = 2x + 1.

screens.title: Basic substitution done.
screens.body: That was the standard linear/quadratic case. TMUA questions often make the same idea harder by hiding the variables inside powers or logarithms.

screens.title: Look for a hidden substitution
screens.body: Some simultaneous equations look difficult because the variables appear inside powers or logarithms. The trick is to replace repeated expressions with simpler letters.
screens.keyPoint: If you see 2<sup>x</sup> and 2<sup>y</sup> repeatedly, try X = 2<sup>x</sup> and Y = 2<sup>y</sup>. Then the system may become ordinary algebra.

screens.title: Use substitution with powers
screens.question: The simultaneous equations are 3<sup>x</sup> + 3<sup>y+1</sup> = 18 and 3<sup>x+y</sup> = 27. Find the value of 2x + y.

steps.title: Rewrite both equations
steps.body: Since 3<sup>y+1</sup> = 3Y and 3<sup>x+y</sup> = XY, the equations become X + 3Y = 18 and XY = 27.

steps.title: Solve for Y
steps.body: Multiplying by Y gives 3Y<sup>2</sup> - 18Y + 27 = 0, so (Y - 3)<sup>2</sup> = 0 and Y = 3.

steps.title: Return to x and y
steps.body: Y = 3<sup>y</sup> = 3, so y = 1. Then X = 9, so 3<sup>x</sup> = 9 and x = 2.

screens.finalAnswer: Therefore 2x + y = 2(2) + 1 = 5, so the correct answer is C.

screens.title: Logs can hide simultaneous equations too

steps.title: Substitute
steps.body: Substitute y = 3x - 1 into 2(y - 3) = x<sup>2</sup>. This gives 2(3x - 4) = x<sup>2</sup>.

steps.title: Solve the quadratic
steps.body: x<sup>2</sup> - 6x + 8 = 0, so (x - 2)(x - 4) = 0. Therefore x = 2 or x = 4.

steps.title: Find y
steps.body: Using y = 3x - 1: if x = 2, y = 5. If x = 4, y = 11.

screens.question: Given log₁₀4 + log₁₀(y - 1) = 2log₁₀x and log₁₀(y + 3 - 2x) = 0, what are the possible values of x?
screens.prompt: Use the log laws to turn both equations into algebraic equations, then substitute.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The first equation becomes 4(y - 1) = x<sup>2</sup>. The second equation gives y + 3 - 2x = 1, so y = 2x - 2. Substituting gives 4(2x - 3) = x<sup>2</sup>, so x<sup>2</sup> - 8x + 12 = 0. Therefore (x - 2)(x - 6) = 0, giving x = 2 or x = 6.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Combine the logarithms in the first equation to get 4(y - 1) = x<sup>2</sup>. The second equation gives y = 2x - 2. Substitute this into the first equation and solve the resulting quadratic.

screens.title: Functions and Simultaneous Equations: key points
keyPoints: Composite functions are substitutions done in a specific order.
f(g(x)) and g(f(x)) are usually different.
A linear/quadratic simultaneous system can be solved by substitution.
When powers repeat, use substitutions like X = 2<sup>x</sup> and Y = 2<sup>y</sup>.
Difference of squares can appear after power substitutions.
Use log laws to turn logarithmic simultaneous equations into algebraic equations.
log₁₀(A) = 0 means A = 1.
Always substitute back to find the actual variable values.
reviewItems: Practise composite functions in both orders.
Practise linear/quadratic simultaneous equations.
Practise substitutions such as X = 2<sup>x</sup> and Y = 2<sup>y</sup>.
Practise turning log equations into algebraic equations using log laws.
Next lesson: Modulus Functions.

screens.title: Modulus represents distance
screens.body: The modulus of a number is its distance from zero. Since distance cannot be negative, |x| is always greater than or equal to zero.
screens.keyPoint: |x| = x when x ≥ 0, but |x| = -x when x < 0. For example, |5| = 5 and |-5| = 5.

screens.title: Remove modulus signs by checking the sign

steps.title: Look inside the modulus
steps.body: The sign of the expression inside the modulus determines whether it stays the same or changes sign.

steps.title: When the expression is non-negative
steps.body: If x - 3 ≥ 0, then x ≥ 3 and |x - 3| = x - 3.

steps.title: When the expression is negative
steps.body: If x - 3 < 0, then x < 3 and |x - 3| = -(x - 3) = 3 - x.

steps.title: Piecewise form
steps.body: Therefore |x - 3| = x - 3 when x ≥ 3, and |x - 3| = 3 - x when x < 3.

screens.question: Given that x < -2, which expression is equal to |x + 2| + |x - 1|?
screens.prompt: Decide whether each expression inside the modulus is positive or negative.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since x < -2, both x + 2 and x - 1 are negative. Therefore |x + 2| = -(x + 2) = -x - 2, and |x - 1| = -(x - 1) = -x + 1. Adding gives (-x - 2) + (-x + 1) = -2x - 1.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Since x < -2, both expressions inside the modulus signs are negative. Therefore |x + 2| = -x - 2 and |x - 1| = -x + 1. Their sum is -2x - 1.

screens.title: The meaning of modulus is clear.
screens.body: You can now use the sign of an expression to remove its modulus signs. Next, we will see what modulus does to a graph.

screens.title: The graph of y = |f(x)| cannot go below the x-axis
screens.body: Taking the modulus of a function changes every negative output into its positive counterpart. Positive parts of the original graph stay where they are, while negative parts are reflected in the x-axis.
screens.keyPoint: For y = |f(x)|, keep the parts where f(x) ≥ 0 and reflect the parts where f(x) < 0 upwards.

screens.title: Sketch y = |x² - 4| from y = x² - 4

steps.title: Identify the negative section
steps.body: The graph of y = x<sup>2</sup> - 4 lies below the x-axis between -2 and 2.

steps.title: Keep the positive sections
steps.body: For x ≤ -2 and x ≥ 2, the original graph is already non-negative, so these sections stay unchanged.

steps.title: Reflect the negative section
steps.body: Between -2 and 2, replace x<sup>2</sup> - 4 with -(x<sup>2</sup> - 4) = 4 - x<sup>2</sup>.

screens.question: Which statement correctly describes the graph of y = |x<sup>2</sup> - 4|?
options: It is y = x² - 4 for every value of x
It is y = 4 - x² for every value of x
It is y = 4 - x² when -2 < x < 2 and y = x² - 4 otherwise
It is y = x² + 4 when -2 < x < 2
The whole graph is reflected in the x-axis
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The expression x<sup>2</sup> - 4 is negative between its roots -2 and 2, so this section is reflected upwards and becomes 4 - x<sup>2</sup>. Outside that interval, x<sup>2</sup> - 4 is non-negative and remains unchanged.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Find where x<sup>2</sup> - 4 is negative. Its roots are -2 and 2, and the quadratic is negative between them. Therefore the middle section becomes 4 - x<sup>2</sup>, while the sections outside remain x<sup>2</sup> - 4.

screens.title: Modulus graphs handled.
screens.body: You can now construct y = |f(x)| by reflecting only the negative outputs. Next, we will solve modulus equations.

screens.title: A modulus equation usually creates two cases
screens.body: If |A| = k and k is positive, then A may equal k or -k. This is because both k and -k are the same distance from zero.
screens.keyPoint: |A| = k gives A = k or A = -k when k ≥ 0. If k is negative, the equation has no solutions.

screens.title: Solve |2x - 3| = 5

steps.title: Create the positive case
steps.body: The expression inside the modulus may equal 5, so 2x - 3 = 5.

steps.title: Create the negative case
steps.body: The expression may instead equal -5, so 2x - 3 = -5.

screens.question: What is the complete set of solutions of |3x + 1| = 7?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Split the equation into two cases. First, 3x + 1 = 7, giving 3x = 6 and x = 2. Second, 3x + 1 = -7, giving 3x = -8 and x = -8/3. Both values satisfy the original equation.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A positive modulus equation creates two cases: 3x + 1 = 7 or 3x + 1 = -7. These give x = 2 and x = -8/3.

screens.title: Modulus equations solved.
screens.body: You can now split a modulus equation into its positive and negative cases. The final step is applying the same distance idea to inequalities.

screens.title: Modulus inequalities describe distances
screens.body: The inequality |x - a| < r means that the distance between x and a is less than r. This produces an interval centred at a.
screens.keyPoint: |x - a| < r means a - r < x < a + r. In contrast, |x - a| > r means x < a - r or x > a + r.

screens.title: Solve |2x - 1| ≤ 5

steps.title: Add one throughout
steps.body: Adding 1 to all three parts gives -4 ≤ 2x ≤ 6.

steps.title: Divide throughout
steps.body: Dividing all three parts by 2 gives -2 ≤ x ≤ 3.

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.body: The solutions form the closed interval from -2 to 3 because equality is allowed.

screens.question: Which set of values satisfies |3x + 2| > 4?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. For |3x + 2| > 4, the expression must lie outside the interval from -4 to 4. Therefore 3x + 2 > 4 or 3x + 2 < -4. The first gives x > 2/3. The second gives x < -2. Hence x < -2 or x > 2/3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Because the modulus is greater than 4, split into two outside cases: 3x + 2 > 4 or 3x + 2 < -4. These simplify to x > 2/3 or x < -2.

screens.title: Modulus Functions: key points
keyPoints: Modulus represents distance, so its value is never negative.
Remove modulus signs by considering whether the expression inside is positive or negative.
For y = |f(x)|, reflect negative parts of y = f(x) in the x-axis.
An equation |A| = k usually creates the two cases A = k and A = -k.
An inequality |x - a| < r describes values inside an interval centred at a.
An inequality |x - a| > r describes values outside that interval.
reviewItems: Practise writing modulus expressions in piecewise form.
Practise reflecting the negative sections of function graphs.
Practise checking both cases in modulus equations.
Practise distinguishing inside and outside modulus inequalities.
Next lesson: Algebra exam-style questions.

screens.question: Let a > 0 and suppose that a1/3 + a-1/3 = 3. Find the value of a + 1a
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Let u = a1/3. Then u + 1u = 3. Cubing gives (u + 1u)3 = u3 + 1u3 + 3(u + 1u). Therefore 27 = a + 1a + 9, so a + 1a = 18.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Let u = a1/3, so u + 1u = 3. Use the identity (u + 1u)3 = u3 + 1u3 + 3(u + 1u). This gives 27 = a + 1a + 9, so the answer is 18.

screens.question: Simplify (√5 + √3√5 - √3)2.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Rationalising first gives √5 + √3√5 - √3 = (√5 + √3)25 - 3 = 8 + 2√152 = 4 + √15. Squaring gives (4 + √15)2 = 16 + 8√15 + 15 = 31 + 8√15.
screens.incorrectFeedback: First rationalise the fraction by multiplying the numerator and denominator by √5 + √3. The fraction simplifies to 4 + √15. You must then square this result: (4 + √15)<sup>2</sup> = 31 + 8√15.

screens.question: For which values of k does the equation x<sup>2</sup> - 2kx + k + 2 = 0 have two distinct positive real roots?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The quadratic formula gives x = k ± √(k<sup>2</sup> - k - 2). For two distinct real roots, k<sup>2</sup> - k - 2 > 0, so (k - 2)(k + 1) > 0. Therefore k < -1 or k > 2. If k < -1, the root k - √(k<sup>2</sup> - k - 2) is negative. If k > 2, both roots are positive because k > √(k<sup>2</sup> - k - 2). Therefore k > 2.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the quadratic formula: x = k ± √(k<sup>2</sup> - k - 2). The discriminant condition gives k < -1 or k > 2. However, k < -1 produces negative roots. When k > 2, even the smaller root k - √(k<sup>2</sup> - k - 2) is positive. Therefore the answer is k > 2.

screens.question: The polynomial p(x) = x<sup>3</sup> + ax<sup>2</sup> + bx + 6 is divisible by both x - 1 and x + 2. What is the remainder when p(x) is divided by x - 3?
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Since x - 1 is a factor, p(1) = 0, giving a + b = -7. Since x + 2 is a factor, p(-2) = 0, giving 2a - b = 1. Solving these equations gives a = -2 and b = -5. The remainder after division by x - 3 is p(3) = 27 - 18 - 15 + 6 = 0.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use the factor theorem twice. From p(1) = 0, obtain a + b = -7. From p(-2) = 0, obtain 2a - b = 1. This gives a = -2 and b = -5. The required remainder is p(3), which equals 0.

screens.question: Let f(x) = |x - 2| + |x + 1|. What is the minimum value of f(x)?
options: 3, attained for every x such that -1 ≤ x ≤ 2
4, attained for every x such that -1 ≤ x ≤ 2
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. For -1 ≤ x ≤ 2, we have |x - 2| = 2 - x and |x + 1| = x + 1. Therefore f(x) = (2 - x) + (x + 1) = 3 throughout the whole interval. Outside this interval, the total is greater than 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Consider the interval between the two critical values, -1 and 2. For -1 ≤ x ≤ 2, |x - 2| = 2 - x and |x + 1| = x + 1. Their sum is always 3, so the minimum is attained throughout -1 ≤ x ≤ 2.

screens.question: The graphs y = x<sup>2</sup> - 4x + 5 and y = mx + 1 meet at exactly one point. What are the possible values of m?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. At an intersection, the two expressions for y are equal, so x2 - 4x + 5 = mx + 1. Rearranging gives x2 - (m + 4)x + 4 = 0.

The graphs meet at exactly one point only when this quadratic has one repeated root, so its discriminant is zero:
(m + 4)2 - 4(1)(4) = 0.

Therefore (m + 4)2 = 16, so m + 4 = ±4. Hence m = 0 or m = -8.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Set the two expressions for y equal:
x2 - 4x + 5 = mx + 1.

This rearranges to x2 - (m + 4)x + 4 = 0. Exactly one intersection means this quadratic must have a repeated root, so its discriminant equals zero:
(m + 4)2 - 16 = 0.

Thus m + 4 = ±4, giving m = 0 or m = -8.

screens.question: A polynomial p(x) leaves remainders 4, 6 and 9 when divided by x - 1, x + 1 and x - 2 respectively. What is the remainder when p(x) is divided by (x - 1)(x + 1)(x - 2)?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The divisor has degree 3, so the remainder must have degree less than 3. Write it as R(x) = ax2 + bx + c.

The remainder theorem gives:
R(1) = a + b + c = 4,
R(-1) = a - b + c = 6,
R(2) = 4a + 2b + c = 9.

Subtracting the first two equations gives 2b = -2, so b = -1. Then a + c = 5. Using R(2) = 9 gives 4a + c = 11. Subtracting a + c = 5 gives 3a = 6, so a = 2 and c = 3.

Therefore the remainder is 2x2 - x + 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Because the divisor has degree 3, the remainder has the form R(x) = ax2 + bx + c.

Use the given remainders:
a + b + c = 4,
a - b + c = 6,
4a + 2b + c = 9.

The first two equations give b = -1. They also give a + c = 5. Substituting b = -1 into the third equation gives 4a + c = 11. Hence a = 2 and c = 3, so R(x) = 2x2 - x + 3.

screens.question: The function f is defined by f(x) = x + 3x - 1, x ≠ 1. What is f(f(x))?
options: It is undefined for every x
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The rule f takes any input t and returns t + 3t - 1. Therefore, when the input is f(x),

f(f(x)) = f(x) + 3f(x) - 1.

Now simplify the top and bottom separately:
f(x) + 3 = x + 3x - 1 + 3 = 4xx - 1,

f(x) - 1 = x + 3x - 1 - 1 = 4x - 1.

So f(f(x)) = 4xx - 1 ÷ 4x - 1 = x. Applying f twice returns the original input.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Remember that f(f(x)) means putting the whole expression f(x) back into the function.

The rule is f(t) = t + 3t - 1, so
f(f(x)) = f(x) + 3f(x) - 1.

Here, f(x) + 3 = 4xx - 1 and f(x) - 1 = 4x - 1. Dividing these expressions gives x.

screens.question: The equation √(x + 5) - √(x - 1) = 2 has one real solution. What is its value?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. First rearrange so one square root is isolated:
√(x + 5) = 2 + √(x - 1).

Square both sides:
x + 5 = 4 + 4√(x - 1) + x - 1.

This simplifies to 2 = 4√(x - 1), so √(x - 1) = 12. Squaring again gives x - 1 = 14, so x = 54.

Checking: √(254) - √(14) = 52 - 12 = 2.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Isolate one square root first:
√(x + 5) = 2 + √(x - 1).

Squaring gives x + 5 = x + 3 + 4√(x - 1). Therefore 2 = 4√(x - 1), so √(x - 1) = 12.

Hence x - 1 = 14, giving x = 54.

screens.question: How many distinct real solutions does the equation |x<sup>2</sup> - 4| = x have?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since |x2 - 4| is non-negative, any solution must satisfy x ≥ 0.

Case 1: x2 - 4 ≥ 0. Then x2 - 4 = x, so x2 - x - 4 = 0. Its roots are 1 ± √172. Only the positive root is valid, and it is greater than 2, so this case gives one solution.

Case 2: x2 - 4 < 0. Then 4 - x2 = x, so x2 + x - 4 = 0. Its roots are -1 ± √172. Only the positive root is valid, and it lies between 0 and 2, so this case gives one more solution.

Therefore there are 2 distinct real solutions.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
The right-hand side must be non-negative, so x ≥ 0. Now split the modulus into two cases.

If x2 - 4 ≥ 0, solve x2 - x - 4 = 0. Exactly one root satisfies x ≥ 2.

If x2 - 4 < 0, solve x2 + x - 4 = 0. Exactly one root satisfies 0 ≤ x < 2.

That gives 2 valid solutions in total.

screens.question: The positive numbers x and y satisfy log2x + log2y = 6 and logxy = 12. What is the value of x - y?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Use the addition law for logarithms:

log2x + log2y = log2(xy) = 6.

Therefore xy = 26 = 64.

The second equation, logxy = 12, means y = x1/2 = √x.

Substituting y = √x into xy = 64 gives x√x = 64, so x3/2 = 64. Raising both sides to the power 23 gives x = 16. Therefore y = √16 = 4, and x - y = 16 - 4 = 12.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
First combine the logarithms:

log2x + log2y = log2(xy) = 6,

so xy = 64. Also, logxy = 12 means y = √x. Substituting gives x√x = 64, or x3/2 = 64. Hence x = 16 and y = 4, so x - y = 12.

screens.question: Which set contains all the real solutions of 3<sup>x+1</sup> + 3<sup>1-x</sup> = 10?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Let t = 3x. Since 3x is positive, t > 0.

Then 3x+1 = 3t and 31-x = 3t. The equation becomes:

3t + 3t = 10.

Multiplying by t gives 3t2 - 10t + 3 = 0. Factorising gives (3t - 1)(t - 3) = 0, so t = 13 or t = 3.

Therefore 3x = 3-1 or 3x = 31, giving x = -1 or x = 1.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Use the substitution t = 3x. Then 3x+1 = 3t and 31-x = 3t.

The equation becomes 3t + 3t = 10. Multiplying through by t and factorising gives (3t - 1)(t - 3) = 0. Hence t = 13 or t = 3, so x = -1 or x = 1.

screens.question: The set S is the complete set of values of x satisfying both inequalities x<sup>2</sup> - 9x + 14 < 0 and 3x + 1 > 13. Which single inequality also represents S?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Factorise the first inequality:

x2 - 9x + 14 = (x - 2)(x - 7).

The quadratic is negative between its roots, so the first inequality gives 2 < x < 7.

The second inequality gives 3x + 1 > 13, so 3x > 12 and therefore x > 4.

Both conditions must hold, so S is 4 < x < 7. The quadratic with roots 4 and 7 is (x - 4)(x - 7). It is negative between its roots, so S can be written as (x - 4)(x - 7) < 0. Expanding gives x2 - 11x + 28 < 0.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Solve the two inequalities separately. The first factorises to (x - 2)(x - 7) < 0, giving 2 < x < 7. The second gives x > 4.

The intersection is therefore 4 < x < 7. A positive quadratic is negative between its roots, so use (x - 4)(x - 7) < 0. Expanding gives x2 - 11x + 28 < 0.

screens.question: The function f is defined by f(x) = x<sup>2</sup> - 6x + 11,   x ≥ 3. Which expression gives f<sup>-1</sup>(x)?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. First complete the square:

f(x) = x2 - 6x + 11 = (x - 3)2 + 2.

Write y = (x - 3)2 + 2. Rearranging gives (x - 3)2 = y - 2.

Because the original domain is x ≥ 3, we know x - 3 ≥ 0. We must therefore take the positive square root:

x - 3 = √(y - 2).

Hence x = 3 + √(y - 2). Replacing y with x gives f-1(x) = 3 + √(x - 2).
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Complete the square first:

f(x) = (x - 3)2 + 2.

Set y = (x - 3)2 + 2 and rearrange to obtain x - 3 = ±√(y - 2). The original restriction x ≥ 3 means x - 3 must be non-negative, so take the positive square root. Therefore f-1(x) = 3 + √(x - 2).

screens.question: The polynomial p(x) = x<sup>3</sup> + ax<sup>2</sup> + bx + 4 is divisible by (x - 1)<sup>2</sup>. What is the value of p(-1)?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since p(x) is a monic cubic and is divisible by (x - 1)2, it must have the form:

p(x) = (x - 1)2(x + c)

for some constant c. From the original expression, p(0) = 4. From the factorised expression, p(0) = (-1)2c = c. Therefore c = 4.

So p(x) = (x - 1)2(x + 4). Substituting x = -1 gives p(-1) = (-2)2(3) = 4 × 3 = 12.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Because the cubic is monic and contains the repeated factor (x - 1)2, write p(x) = (x - 1)2(x + c).

The constant term is p(0) = c, but the question tells us the constant term is 4. Therefore c = 4. Now substitute x = -1 into (x - 1)2(x + 4), giving (-2)2(3) = 12.

screens.question: For which values of k is x<sup>2</sup> + 2kx + k + 3 > 0 for every real value of x?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The coefficient of x2 is positive, so the graph opens upwards. For the expression to be strictly positive for every real x, the graph must never touch or cross the x-axis. Its discriminant must therefore be negative.

(2k)2 - 4(1)(k + 3) < 0.

This simplifies to 4k2 - 4k - 12 < 0, and hence k2 - k - 3 < 0.

The boundary values are found from k2 - k - 3 = 0, giving k = 1 ± √132. Since the quadratic in k opens upwards, it is negative between its roots. Therefore 1 - √132 < k < 1 + √132.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
The graph opens upwards because the coefficient of x2 is positive. To be strictly above the x-axis for every x, it must have no real roots, so the discriminant must be negative.

This gives (2k)2 - 4(k + 3) < 0, which simplifies to k2 - k - 3 < 0. The roots are 1 ± √132, and the expression is negative between them.

screens.question: Evaluate (√(5 + 2√6) - √(5 - 2√6))<sup>2</sup>.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Use (a - b)2 = a2 + b2 - 2ab.

The expression becomes:

(5 + 2√6) + (5 - 2√6) - 2√((5 + 2√6)(5 - 2√6)).

The first two terms add to 10. The product inside the square root is a difference of squares:

(5 + 2√6)(5 - 2√6) = 25 - (2√6)2 = 25 - 24 = 1.

Therefore the value is 10 - 2√1 = 10 - 2 = 8.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Expand using (a - b)2 = a2 + b2 - 2ab. The two squared terms add to 10.

For the product, use the conjugates: (5 + 2√6)(5 - 2√6) = 25 - 24 = 1. Therefore the full expression is 10 - 2√1 = 8.

screens.question: How many real solutions does the equation x + 1x - 2 + x - 2x + 1 = 2 have?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. First note that x ≠ 2 and x ≠ -1. Multiply the equation by (x - 2)(x + 1):

(x + 1)2 + (x - 2)2 = 2(x - 2)(x + 1).

Expanding the left-hand side gives 2x2 - 2x + 5. Expanding the right-hand side gives 2x2 - 2x - 4.

The equation would therefore require 2x2 - 2x + 5 = 2x2 - 2x - 4, which simplifies to 5 = -4. This is impossible, so there are no real solutions.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Multiply both sides by (x - 2)(x + 1). This gives (x + 1)2 + (x - 2)2 = 2(x - 2)(x + 1).

After expanding, the left-hand side is 2x2 - 2x + 5, while the right-hand side is 2x2 - 2x - 4. This would imply 5 = -4, so the equation has 0 real solutions.

screens.question: The functions f and g are defined by f(x) = √(2x - 1) and g(x) = 1x - 3. What is the domain of f(g(x))?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Substituting g(x) into f gives:

f(g(x)) = √(2x - 3 - 1).

The expression inside the square root must be non-negative:

2x - 3 - 1 ≥ 0.

Combining the terms gives 5 - xx - 3 ≥ 0. The critical values are x = 3 and x = 5.

x = 3 must be excluded because it makes the denominator zero. A sign check shows the fraction is positive between 3 and 5, while x = 5 is included because it makes the fraction zero. Therefore the domain is 3 < x ≤ 5.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Form the composite function first:

f(g(x)) = √(2x - 3 - 1).

The quantity inside the square root must be at least zero. This gives 5 - xx - 3 ≥ 0. The fraction is non-negative for 3 < x ≤ 5. The value x = 3 is excluded because the denominator would be zero, but x = 5 is included.

screens.question: A number x satisfies x<sup>2</sup> - 5x + 3 = 0. Let t = x<sup>2</sup>. Which equation must t satisfy?
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Since t = x2, the original equation becomes:

t - 5x + 3 = 0.

Rearranging gives 5x = t + 3, so x = t + 35.

But t = x2, so substitute this expression for x:

t = (t + 35)2.

Multiplying by 25 gives 25t = (t + 3)2. Expanding gives 25t = t2 + 6t + 9. Moving everything to one side gives t2 - 19t + 9 = 0.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Use only substitution. Since t = x2, rewrite the original equation as t - 5x + 3 = 0. Therefore x = t + 35.

Now use t = x2:

t = (t + 35)2.

Multiplying out gives 25t = t2 + 6t + 9, so t2 - 19t + 9 = 0.

screens.title: Algebra and Functions: exam practice complete
keyPoints: Index expressions can often be simplified by introducing a substitution.
When rationalising a two-term denominator, multiply by its conjugate.
For parameterised quadratics, check both the number and signs of the roots.
The factor theorem can create simultaneous equations for unknown coefficients.
A sum of distances represented by modulus expressions may be constant across an interval.
reviewItems: Review Question 1 if the cubic identity was difficult to spot.
Review Question 3 if you found the parameter conditions difficult.
Review the difference between the factor theorem and remainder theorem.
Try the questions again without reading the solutions.

screens.title: Two ways to define a sequence
screens.body:
A sequence is an ordered list of numbers. It can be defined directly by a formula for its nth term, or generated one term at a time using a recurrence relation.

For example, uₙ = 3n - 1 defines every term directly. Substituting n = 1, 2, 3, ... gives 2, 5, 8, 11, ...

A recurrence relation instead uses an earlier term to generate the next term.
screens.keyPoint: A recurrence relation normally needs both a rule and a starting value. For example, u₁ = 2 and uₙ₊₁ = 2uₙ - 1 generate the sequence 2, 3, 5, 9, ...

screens.title: Generate terms from a recurrence relation

steps.title: Start with the given first term
steps.body: Suppose u₁ = 4 and uₙ₊₁ = 3uₙ - 2. We begin with u₁ = 4.

steps.title: State the answer
steps.body: Therefore u₄ = 82. Each new term must be found using the term immediately before it.

screens.question: The sequence (xₙ) is defined by x₁ = 3,   xₙ₊₁ = 2xₙ + 1. What is x₄?
screens.prompt: Apply the recurrence relation one term at a time.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Start with x₁ = 3.

x₂ = 2(3) + 1 = 7,
x₃ = 2(7) + 1 = 15,
x₄ = 2(15) + 1 = 31.

Therefore x₄ = 31.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Apply the recurrence repeatedly, beginning with x₁ = 3.

x₂ = 7, x₃ = 15 and x₄ = 31.

The answer is 31.

screens.title: Sequence rules understood.
screens.body:
You can now generate terms from both an nth-term formula and a simple recurrence relation.

Next, we will study sequences whose terms change by a constant difference.

screens.title: Arithmetic progressions have a constant difference
screens.body:
An arithmetic progression is a sequence in which the difference between consecutive terms is constant.

For example, 7, 11, 15, 19, ... has common difference d = 4.
screens.keyPoint: If the first term is a and the common difference is d, then the nth term is uₙ = a + (n - 1)d. The n - 1 appears because no difference has been added at the first term.

screens.title: Find the position of a term

steps.title: Identify the first term and difference
steps.body: Consider the arithmetic progression 8, 13, 18, 23, ... Here a = 8 and d = 5.

steps.title: Set the nth term equal to 103
Interpret the result
steps.body: Therefore 103 is the 20th term of the progression.

screens.question:
An arithmetic progression has third term 11 and eighth term 31.

What is its first term?
screens.prompt: Write an equation for each of the two given terms.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Using uₙ = a + (n - 1)d:

The third term gives a + 2d = 11.
The eighth term gives a + 7d = 31.

Subtracting the equations gives 5d = 20, so d = 4.

Substitute into a + 2d = 11:
a + 8 = 11, so a = 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
The third and eighth terms give:

a + 2d = 11
a + 7d = 31.

Subtracting gives 5d = 20, so d = 4. Substituting back gives a = 3.

screens.title: Arithmetic terms mastered.
screens.body:
You can now identify a common difference, find an nth term and recover unknown information from given terms.

Next, we will add the terms of an arithmetic progression.

screens.title: A series is the sum of sequence terms
screens.body:
A sequence lists terms, while a series adds them.

For an arithmetic progression with first term a, common difference d and n terms, the sum is Sₙ = n2(2a + (n - 1)d). If the final term l is known, the equivalent form is Sₙ = n2(a + l).
screens.keyPoint: For the natural numbers, 1 + 2 + ... + n = n(n + 1)/2.

screens.title: Find an arithmetic-series sum

steps.title: Identify the progression
steps.body: Find 7 + 12 + 17 + ... + 102. The first term is 7 and the common difference is 5.

steps.title: Find the number of terms
steps.body: The nth term is 7 + 5(n - 1). Set this equal to 102.

steps.title: Use the first and final terms

screens.question:
The sum of the first n natural numbers is 210.

What is n?
screens.prompt: Use the formula for 1 + 2 + ... + n.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Use n(n + 1)2 = 210. Therefore n(n + 1) = 420.

Since 20 × 21 = 420, we obtain n = 20.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Use n(n + 1)/2 = 210. Multiplying by 2 gives n(n + 1) = 420.

The consecutive numbers 20 and 21 have product 420, so n = 20.

screens.title: Arithmetic sums complete.
screens.body:
You can now sum finite arithmetic series and use the formula for the sum of the first n natural numbers.

Next, we will study sequences that change by multiplication rather than addition.

screens.title: Geometric progressions have a constant ratio
screens.body:
A geometric progression is a sequence in which each term is obtained by multiplying the previous term by a constant ratio r.

For example, 5, 15, 45, 135, ... has common ratio r = 3.
screens.keyPoint: If the first term is a and the common ratio is r, then uₙ = ar<sup>n-1</sup>. A negative ratio creates an alternating sequence, such as 4, -8, 16, -32, ...

screens.title: Use two terms to find a geometric progression

steps.title: Write the two given terms
steps.body: A geometric progression has second term 12 and fifth term 324. Therefore ar = 12 and ar<sup>4</sup> = 324.

steps.title: Find the positive ratio
steps.body: The question states that the ratio is positive, so r = 3.

steps.title: Find the first term
steps.body: Since ar = 12, we have 3a = 12, so a = 4.

screens.question: The first three terms of a geometric progression are x,   12,   18, where x > 0. What is x?
screens.prompt: The ratio between each pair of consecutive terms must be equal.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The common ratio from 12 to 18 is r = 1812 = 32. Therefore 12 = x(3/2). Multiplying by 2/3 gives x = 8.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
First find the common ratio: 18/12 = 3/2.

Since the second term is obtained by multiplying x by 3/2, we have 12 = 3x/2. Therefore x = 8.

screens.title: Geometric terms mastered.
screens.body:
You can now recognise geometric progressions, work with negative ratios and find unknown terms using uₙ = arn-1.

The final block covers the sum of a finite geometric series.

screens.title: Summing a finite geometric series
screens.body: For a geometric progression with first term a, common ratio r and n terms, the finite sum is Sₙ = a(1 - rn)1 - r, r ≠ 1. An equivalent form is Sₙ = a(rn - 1)r - 1.
screens.keyPoint: Both formulas are equivalent. Choose the version that makes the signs easiest to manage. This lesson only considers a finite number of terms; sum to infinity comes later.

screens.title: Sum a finite geometric series

steps.title: Identify the first term and ratio

screens.question:
A finite geometric series has first term 5, common ratio 2 and sum 635.

How many terms does it contain?
screens.prompt: Substitute the given values into the finite geometric-series formula.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Using the finite geometric-series formula:

Sₙ = 5(2n - 1), because r - 1 = 1.

Therefore 5(2n - 1) = 635.

Divide by 5:
2n - 1 = 127.

Hence 2n = 128 = 27, so n = 7.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Use Sₙ = 5(2n - 1).

Setting this equal to 635 gives 2n - 1 = 127, so 2n = 128.

Since 128 = 27, the series contains 7 terms.

screens.title: Arithmetic and Geometric Progressions: key points
keyPoints: A sequence may be defined by an nth-term formula or generated using a recurrence relation.
A recurrence relation needs a starting value as well as a rule.
For an arithmetic progression, uₙ = a + (n - 1)d.
For an arithmetic series, Sₙ = n(2a + (n - 1)d)/2.
The sum of the first n natural numbers is n(n + 1)/2.
For a geometric progression, uₙ = ar<sup>n-1</sup>.
For a finite geometric series, Sₙ = a(1 - r<sup>n</sup>)/(1 - r).
A negative common ratio creates an alternating geometric progression.
reviewItems: Practise generating terms from recurrence relations.
Practise finding unknown arithmetic terms from two given terms.
Practise deciding which arithmetic-series formula is most convenient.
Practise finding the first term and ratio of a geometric progression.
Practise finding the number of terms before calculating a finite sum.
Next lesson: Binomial Expansion and Factorials.

screens.title: Factorials package repeated multiplication
screens.body:
For a positive integer n, n! means n × (n - 1) × (n - 2) × ... × 2 × 1.

For example, 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120.

We also define 0! = 1.
screens.keyPoint: Factorials often cancel. For example, 8!6! = 8 × 7 = 56, because the factors from 6! downwards cancel.

screens.question: What is 9!7!?
screens.prompt: Cancel the common factorial before multiplying.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. 9! = 9 × 8 × 7!, so 9!7! = 9 × 8 = 72.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Cancel 7! first: 9!7! = 9 × 8 × 7!7! = 9 × 8 = 72.

screens.title: ⁿCᵣ counts the ways to choose r objects
screens.body:
The notation ⁿCᵣ, read 'n choose r', is a binomial coefficient.

ⁿCᵣ = n!(n - r)!r!, where 0 ≤ r ≤ n.

These numbers become the coefficients in a binomial expansion.
screens.keyPoint: The coefficients are symmetric: ⁿCᵣ = ⁿCₙ₋ᵣ. For example, ⁷C₂ = ⁷C₅.

screens.title: Calculate a binomial coefficient
Each term has a predictable structure
screens.body:
For a positive integer n, the expansion of (a + b)n contains n + 1 terms.

The general term is:

Tᵣ₊₁ = ⁿCᵣan-rbr, for r = 0, 1, 2, ..., n.
screens.keyPoint: As r increases, the power of a decreases while the power of b increases. In every term, the two powers add to n.

screens.title: Expand (1 + 2x)⁴

screens.question: What is the coefficient of x<sup>3</sup> in the expansion of (2 - x)<sup>5</sup>?
screens.prompt: Use the term with r = 3 and include the negative sign.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The x<sup>3</sup> term is ⁵C₃(2)<sup>2</sup>(-x)<sup>3</sup> = 10 × 4 × (-x<sup>3</sup>) = -40x<sup>3</sup>.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use r = 3: ⁵C₃(2)<sup>5-3</sup>(-x)<sup>3</sup> = 10 × 4 × (-x<sup>3</sup>) = -40x<sup>3</sup>. The coefficient is -40.

screens.title: Core binomial expansion complete.
screens.body:
You can now use factorials, calculate ⁿCᵣ and select a particular term from an expansion.

Next, we will apply the same method when the second term is a function such as 3x2.

screens.title: Track the power created by f(x)
screens.body:
The same theorem applies to (a + f(x))n.

The general term is ⁿCᵣan-r(f(x))r.

If f(x) = 3x2, then (f(x))r = 3rx2r.
screens.keyPoint: In an expansion involving x<sup>2</sup>, only even powers of x appear. To find the x<sup>6</sup> term, solve 2r = 6, giving r = 3.

screens.title: Find the coefficient of x⁶

steps.title: Choose the correct value of r
steps.body: In (2 + 3x<sup>2</sup>)<sup>5</sup>, the power of x in the rth choice is x<sup>2r</sup>. For x<sup>6</sup>, 2r = 6, so r = 3.

steps.title: Calculate
steps.body: 10 × 4 × 27 = 1080, so the coefficient of x<sup>6</sup> is 1080.

screens.question: Which power of x does not appear in the expansion of (1 + 2x<sup>3</sup>)<sup>6</sup>?
screens.prompt: The powers of x are multiples of 3.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The powers are x<sup>3r</sup> for r = 0, 1, ..., 6, so they are x<sup>0</sup>, x<sup>3</sup>, x<sup>6</sup>, x<sup>9</sup>, x<sup>12</sup>, x<sup>15</sup> and x<sup>18</sup>. Therefore x<sup>14</sup> does not appear.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The power of x is 3r. Since r is an integer from 0 to 6, only multiples of 3 appear. Therefore x<sup>14</sup> is absent.

screens.question:
In the expansion of (a + bx2)7, the coefficient of x10 is 15 times the coefficient of x6.

Given that a and b are non-zero positive integers, what is the smallest possible value of a + b?
screens.prompt: Match x¹⁰ with r = 5 and x⁶ with r = 3.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. The coefficient of x10 is ⁷C₅a2b5 = 21a2b5.

The coefficient of x6 is ⁷C₃a4b3 = 35a4b3.

Therefore 21a2b5 = 15(35a4b3). Dividing by 21a2b3 gives b2 = 25a2.

Since a and b are positive, b = 5a. The smallest choice is a = 1 and b = 5, so a + b = 6.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
The x10 term uses r = 5, while the x6 term uses r = 3.

Set 21a2b5 = 15(35a4b3). This simplifies to b2 = 25a2, so b = 5a.

The smallest positive integers are a = 1 and b = 5, giving a + b = 6.

screens.title: Binomial Expansion and Factorials: key points
keyPoints: The expansion of (a + b)ⁿ has n + 1 terms.
The general term is Tᵣ₊₁ = ⁿCᵣa<sup>n-r</sup>b<sup>r</sup>.
To find a coefficient, first identify the value of r that produces the required power.
For (a + f(x))ⁿ, remember to raise the whole function f(x) to the power r.
The sign of a term matters when the binomial contains subtraction.
reviewItems: Practise cancelling factorials before multiplying.
Practise calculating ⁿCᵣ using the factorial formula.
Practise selecting one coefficient without expanding every term.
Practise matching powers when f(x) contains x² or x³.
Next lesson: Advanced Series Logic.

screens.title: A geometric series converges only when |r| < 1
screens.body:
For a geometric progression with first term a and common ratio r:

Sₙ = a(1 - rn)1 - r.

If |r| < 1, then rn approaches 0 as n becomes large, so the series has a finite sum to infinity.
screens.keyPoint: S∞ = a1 - r, but only when |r| < 1. A negative ratio is allowed provided its magnitude is less than 1.

screens.question: Which geometric progression has a finite sum to infinity?
screens.prompt: Check the magnitude of the common ratio.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The ratio is -2/3, and |-2/3| < 1, so the terms approach 0 and the series converges.
screens.incorrectFeedback: A geometric series converges only when |r| < 1. Of the options, only r = -2/3 satisfies this condition.

screens.title: Use the sums of powers to recover a and r

steps.title: Write the two sums
steps.body:
Suppose the sum of the terms is 4 and the sum of their squares is 48.

a1 - r = 4 and a21 - r2 = 48.

steps.title: Divide to remove a
steps.body:
4842 = 1 - r1 + r.

Therefore 3 = 1 - r1 + r.

steps.title: Find the common ratio and first term
Sum the cubes
steps.body:
The cubes form a GP with first term a3 and ratio r3.

The required sum is 631 - (-1/2)3 = 192.

screens.question:
The sum to infinity of a geometric progression is 8. The sum to infinity of the squares of its terms is 643.

What is the sum to infinity of the cubes of its terms?
screens.prompt: First use the two given sums to recover r.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Dividing the square-sum equation by the square of the original sum gives 1 - r1 + r = 1/3, so r = 1/2.

Then a = 8(1 - 1/2) = 4. The cube sum is 431 - (1/2)3 = 5127.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Let the original sum be S and the square sum be Q. Then QS2 = 1 - r1 + r.

Here this gives 1/3, so r = 1/2 and a = 4. Therefore the cube sum is 5127.

screens.title: Roots repeatedly divide the exponent
screens.body:
Suppose x₁ = 516 and xₙ₊₁ = √xₙ.

Then x₂ = 58, x₃ = 54, x₄ = 52 and x₅ = 5.

It is often easier to track the exponent than to track the whole number.
screens.keyPoint: If x₁ = c<sup>m</sup> and xₙ₊₁ = xₙ<sup>1/k</sup>, then xₙ = c<sup>m/k^(n-1)</sup>.

screens.question:
A sequence is defined by x₁ = 79 and xₙ₊₁ = ∛xₙ.

What is x₆?
screens.prompt: Each step divides the exponent by 3.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. After five recurrence steps, the exponent is 935 = 1/27, so x₆ = 71/27.
screens.incorrectFeedback: From x₁ to x₆ there are five recurrence steps. Each divides the exponent by 3, so the exponent becomes 9/3<sup>5</sup> = 1/27.

screens.title: Infinite sums and recurrence logic mastered.
screens.body:
You can now test convergence, recover a ratio from related infinite sums and simplify root-based recurrence relations.

Next, we will connect different partial sums without expanding every term.

screens.title: Split a long geometric sum into repeated blocks
screens.body:
The first 2n terms can be split into two blocks of n terms.

The second block is rn times the first, so:

S₂ₙ = Sₙ + rnSₙ = Sₙ(1 + rn).
screens.keyPoint: Similarly, S₃ₙ = Sₙ(1 + r<sup>n</sup> + r<sup>2n</sup>). These identities can reveal r without using the full sum formula.

screens.question:
For a geometric progression, the sum of the first 8 terms is 17 times the sum of the first 4 terms. The 9th term is 768.

What is the first term?
screens.prompt: Use S₈ = S₄(1 + r⁴).
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. S₈ = S₄(1 + r4), so 1 + r4 = 17 and r4 = 16.

Therefore r8 = 256. Since the 9th term is ar8 = 768, a = 768/256 = 3.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
Split the first 8 terms into two blocks of 4:

S₈ = S₄(1 + r4). Hence r4 = 16 and r8 = 256.

Now ar8 = 768, so a = 3.

screens.title: The remaining infinite tail is another geometric series

steps.title: Use the tail identity
steps.body:
For a convergent GP:

S∞ - Sₙ = S∞rn.

steps.title: Apply it to an example
steps.body:
Suppose S∞ = 20 and S₃ = 35/2, with r > 0.

The remaining tail is 20 - 35/2 = 5/2.

steps.title: Find a later term
steps.body: Since a = 20(1 - 1/2) = 10, the 5th term is ar<sup>4</sup> = 10/16 = 5/8.

screens.question:
The first, second and third terms of a convergent geometric progression are also the first, fifth and seventh terms, respectively, of an arithmetic progression.

The sum to infinity of the geometric progression is 14. What is its first term?
screens.prompt: Express the arithmetic common difference using the first two matched terms.
screens.correctFeedback:
Correct. Let the GP have first term a and ratio r, and let the AP have common difference d.

a + 4d = ar, so d = a(r - 1)/4.

Also a + 6d = ar2. Substituting for d gives 2r2 - 3r + 1 = 0, so r = 1 or r = 1/2.

The GP is convergent, so r = 1/2. Hence 14 = a/(1 - 1/2) = 2a, giving a = 7.
screens.incorrectFeedback:
From a + 4d = ar, we get d = a(r - 1)/4. Substitute this into a + 6d = ar2.

This gives 2r2 - 3r + 1 = 0, so r = 1 or 1/2. Convergence rules out r = 1.

Therefore 14 = 2a, so the first term is 7.

screens.title: Advanced Series Logic: key points
keyPoints: A geometric series has a finite sum to infinity only when |r| < 1.
For a convergent GP, S∞ = a / (1 - r).
The squares and cubes of GP terms form new GPs with ratios r² and r³.
Root-based recurrence relations are often simplest when you track exponents.
S₂ₙ = Sₙ(1 + rⁿ), which links partial sums without full expansion.
The infinite tail satisfies S∞ - Sₙ = S∞rⁿ.
When a question links arithmetic and geometric progressions, translate each statement into equations before solving.
reviewItems: Practise deciding whether a geometric series converges.
Practise recovering a and r from two related sums.
Practise block identities involving Sₙ, S₂ₙ and S₃ₙ.
Practise recurrence relations involving square and cube roots.
Practise mixed arithmetic-geometric progression problems.

screens.question: Adding Log(A) and Log(B) gives Log(AB)
screens.correctFeedback: Exactly. Log(A) + Log(B) = Log(AB)
screens.incorrectFeedback: When adding two logarithms of the same base, you can simply take the logarithm of the product
